answers: Short answer / triage summary - Most likely root cause for the "walled off" folder: a Sensitivity Label with encryption was applied after the folder had unique SharePoint permissions, and the label's allowed principals did not match the folder ACL (label -> Compliance_Core; ACL -> COMPLIANCE_TEAM). That produces decryption failures even when SharePoint ACLs appear to allow access, and token caching can make access appear intermittent (Incognito shows fresh tokens so it can look like a "fix"). [Data: Sources (30); Sources (31); Reports (63)] - The external auditor's upload hanging is consistent with an authentication/decryption failure (the remote client times out while our service cannot complete authorization/decryption for the upload). WinSCP reproducing hang->fail was seen in the investigation. [Data: Sources (34); Reports (8)] - The Teams camera and microphone symptoms look independent and client‑side: missing camera option despite Device Manager showing hardware strongly points at OS privacy settings, Teams cache/permission issues or drivers; a mic showing selected but no input level suggests headset/driver/exclusive‑mode or Teams audio process issues. Triage those separately in parallel. [Data: Sources (42); Sources (37)] What to do right now (short checklist you can run immediately) 1. Force fresh tokens for affected users: have everyone fully sign out of Office and browsers, then sign back in. If you can, have an admin revoke refresh tokens for the affected accounts so clients must get new tokens. This often converts intermittent access into a reproducible result. [Data: Sources (31); Reports (8)] 2. Quick test to prove the label->group theory: add one affected user temporarily to the AAD group the label allows (Compliance_Core) and test access. In the incident this immediate step restored access for a test user. If it works, you've confirmed the label/group mismatch. (Do this only as a temporary test and document it.) [Data: Sources (30)] 3. Ask Compliance to check the label settings in Purview: confirm the label name ("Confidential - Internal"), the encryption recipients (Compliance_Core), and whether "Preserve custom permissions" was enabled when the label was published - the investigation explicitly found it was not enabled. If the preserve option is appropriate, reapply in test first. [Data: Sources (30); Sources (31); Reports (63)] 4. If business‑critical and safe to do so: consider temporarily removing the label (or expanding its allowed principals) while you fix the group/object mapping - coordinate with Compliance because this affects encryption rights. The incident remediation included remove -> reapply with corrected mapping. [Data: Sources (31); Reports (60, 8)] Evidence to collect (what to ask users / auditor to provide) - From affected browser sessions: a HAR (Network) capture for both normal and Incognito reproductions, plus screenshots of the error (URL visible) and timestamp (UTC). The normal vs Incognito comparison was diagnostic in the investigation. [Data: Sources (30); Sources (38)] - From Identity/Engineering: Azure AD sign‑in logs for affected UPNs (time range around the failures) to inspect token/group claims (look whether tokens include COMPLIANCE_TEAM but not Compliance_Core). The ticket notes show sign‑in log analysis found exactly that claim mismatch. [Data: Sources (31); Reports (8)] - From Compliance admin: a screenshot/export of the Purview label Encryption/Protection settings showing the allowed group(s) and the state of "Preserve custom permissions." [Data: Sources (31); Reports (63)] - From SharePoint: screenshot of the folder's Manage access / unique permissions showing which group object is in the ACL (SP_Compliance_Main / COMPLIANCE_TEAM). [Data: Sources (30)] - From the external auditor: WinSCP session logs and, if possible, a packet capture (pcap) or edge/firewall logs for the failed attempt (timestamped). In prior cases WinSCP + pcap proved whether the failure was network/TLS or auth/decrypt. [Data: Sources (34)] Teams device problems - immediate triage steps - Camera missing in Teams (desktop and browser): check Windows Privacy -> Camera / Microphone to ensure Teams is allowed; clear Teams cache (exit Teams, delete %appdata%\Microsoft\Teams\Cache), sign back in; try Teams web in Incognito to rule out desktop client permissions. These steps were used successfully in similar incidents. [Data: Sources (42)] - Microphone selected but no input level (USB headset): test in Windows Sound control panel (check that headset is default device, check levels, try Windows "Test" and Troubleshoot); reseat cable / try another USB port (ticket recounts a cable reseat fixed a similar mic case); try a different headset or host to exclude hardware/firmware. If device is OK but Teams shows zero input, collect Teams logs (Ctrl+Shift+Alt+1) and escalate to UC engineering. [Data: Sources (37); Sources (42)] Where to escalate / classification guidance - Treat the SharePoint + sensitivity‑label + AAD group/object problem as 2nd‑level escalation (M365/Identity + Compliance/Purview). This crosses identity, Purview encryption and SharePoint ACLs and requires group/objectId reconciliation and possibly an Azure AD Connect sync, which are beyond standard 1st‑line actions. The investigation escalated this to BU Infrastructure / system engineers. [Data: Sources (30); Reports (8); Sources (13)] - The external upload failing should involve Network/Security in parallel (collect pcap and firewall/WAF logs during a coordinated test window) because authentication/decryption failures must be correlated with TCP/TLS behavior to rule network vs identity causes. [Data: Sources (34); Reports (13)] - Teams camera/mic: start 1st‑level device triage (OS permissions, cache, drivers, headset cable) and escalate to 2nd‑level UC engineering only if client logs and device checks don't resolve it. In past tickets camera permission fixes were handled at 1st level and mic driver/hardware fixes were resolved at that level before escalating. [Data: Sources (42); Sources (37)] Recommended short action plan for the next 24 hours 1. (Immediate) Ask Compliance to pause any label changes and provide a screenshot of the label's encryption principals and the "Preserve custom permissions" state. [Data: Sources (31)] 2. (Immediate) Have affected users sign out of all Office/browser sessions; test access in Incognito and capture HARs/screenshots. Send one test user to be added temporarily to Compliance_Core to prove fix. [Data: Sources (30); Sources (38)] 3. (Within 2 hours) Identity team: pull Azure AD sign‑in logs for a sample affected user around the failure time and inspect token group claims; compare success vs failure tokens. [Data: Sources (31)] 4. (Parallel) Ask auditor to reproduce failure while capturing WinSCP logs and a pcap, and schedule Network/Security to run perimeter/internal captures during that window. [Data: Sources (34)] 5. (Teams) Run device checks (privacy settings, Teams cache clear, Teams logs) and collect the Teams logs from the affected users for escalation if device fixes fail. [Data: Sources (42); Sources (37)] If you want, I can: - Draft the exact queries / KQL / Graph or PowerShell snippets to extract sign‑in logs, group objectIds and Purview label exports for your admins to run; or - Produce copy/paste messages to send to the external auditor (what logs/pcap to capture) and to internal users (HAR + screenshots + exact time format), and templates for Compliance to screenshot the Purview label page. Which would you like me to prepare next? (I recommend the sign‑in log + Purview screenshot + one HAR from a failing normal session and one HAR from Incognito as the top priority for quick diagnosis.)
